title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a coastal clean-up project. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Iris, The Tide Pool Mapper
Iris first volunteered with a coastal clean-up group after noticing that most litter counts still ignored the small rock pools where children liked to explore. [47]Her biology degree helped her identify which pools contained fragile creatures and therefore urgently needed volunteers to step around them, not through them.[/47] She expected the work to feel peaceful, but timing each session around the tide made everything surprisingly tense. [48]The hardest morning came when a sudden fog made the team lose sight of their marked bags along the beach.[/48] Iris began carrying brighter flags after that. She was proud when local teachers asked to use her simple map in science lessons. What changed her view most was realising that removing rubbish was only half the job; recording where it appeared made the group more persuasive when speaking to businesses. She still keeps one rusted toy car on her desk as a reminder.
texts.B:
### Ben, The Kayak Collector
Ben joined the harbour clean-up because he already owned a battered kayak and liked being alone on the water before breakfast. [49]He was first contacted by the harbourmaster, who needed someone able to reach rubbish trapped under the old wooden pontoons.[/49] Ben wrongly assumed floating bottles would be easy to grab, but the currents pushed them away whenever he stretched too far. He quickly learned to work in short circles rather than straight lines. [50]His most useful discovery was that tying a laundry basket to the kayak gave him a safer place to drop sharp cans and broken plastic.[/50] Other volunteers laughed at the basket until they saw how much it held. Ben says the strangest part was being cheered by tourists on the pier while doing something messy and unglamorous. He now trains newcomers to judge wind direction before launching, because enthusiasm alone can put them in danger.
texts.C:
### Carla, The Campaign Photographer
Carla offered to photograph the beach clean-up for social media, thinking she would stay dry and simply record other people’s efforts. [51]She soon realised that close-up pictures of ordinary items, such as bottle tops and cigarette ends, made the pollution feel more personal than wide shots of full bags.[/51] Her background in wedding photography helped her notice hands, expressions and small moments of teamwork. [52]She was embarrassed when her expensive camera stopped working after wind blew sand into the lens during the first event.[/52] After that, she borrowed a waterproof compact camera and relaxed. Carla’s images were later used by the council in a campaign about single-use packaging. She felt especially proud of a picture showing a child holding a tiny piece of blue plastic beside a matching sweet wrapper. For Carla, the clean-up proved that persuasion can begin with one carefully framed detail.
texts.D:
### Dev, The Data Volunteer
Dev did not enjoy beaches much, but he loved spreadsheets and careful lists, and joined when the clean-up group admitted that nobody was analysing its results. [53]He created a coding system so volunteers could record whether rubbish came from fishing, picnics, shipping, household waste or tourism.[/53] At first, several people complained that his form was too detailed, so he reduced it to one page. [54]He was surprised by how emotional he felt when older residents described swimming there before the water became polluted.[/54] Their stories suddenly made the numbers matter to him. Dev also noticed that weekend clean-ups produced different results from weekday ones, which later helped the group choose better survey dates. [55]He now insists that every new volunteer spends ten minutes learning the categories before picking anything up.[/55] [56]His proudest achievement was producing a clear chart that convinced a nearby café to stop using plastic stirrers.[/56]

questions.question: says their academic knowledge helped protect delicate wildlife during the clean-up?
questions.explanation: Iris used her biology degree to identify fragile rock pools volunteers should avoid stepping through.

questions.question: recalls a weather problem that made it hard to find equipment on the beach?
questions.explanation: Iris says fog made the team lose sight of their marked bags.

questions.question: was approached because they could reach rubbish from the water?
questions.explanation: Ben was contacted by the harbourmaster because he could reach rubbish under the pontoons.

questions.question: invented a practical way to collect dangerous objects more safely?
questions.explanation: Ben tied a laundry basket to the kayak for sharp cans and broken plastic.

questions.question: realised that photographing small individual objects could make the issue more powerful?
questions.explanation: Carla found that close-up pictures of ordinary litter made pollution feel more personal.

questions.question: had a piece of equipment damaged by conditions at the site?
questions.explanation: Carla’s camera stopped working after sand blew into the lens.

questions.question: developed a system for classifying different sources of waste?
questions.explanation: Dev created categories for recording whether rubbish came from fishing, picnics, shipping, household waste or tourism.

questions.question: was unexpectedly affected by older people’s memories of the place?
questions.explanation: Dev was surprised by his emotional reaction to older residents’ stories.

questions.question: requires newcomers to learn the recording categories before starting work?
questions.explanation: Dev insists that new volunteers learn the categories before picking anything up.

questions.question: produced evidence that persuaded a business to change one of its habits?
questions.explanation: Dev’s chart convinced a café to stop using plastic stirrers.
